utterances: Drop us an email: talk@npr.org.
And you could read what other listeners have to say at out blog at npr.org/blogofthenation.
Rick Steves is the author of several European travel guide books.
He hosts the public radio talk show ""Travel with Rick Steves"" and the television show ""Rick Steves' Europe."" And he joins us now from his - from a studio in Edmonds, Washington.
And it's nice to have you in TALK OF THE NATION today.
Thanks, Neal.
Good to be with you.
And, all right, we talked in the previous segment about how to get to Europe at a reasonable price.
Where should you go to have a reasonably priced vacation once you get there?
Well, that's the natural question with the dollar, where it is these days?
Where are all my dollars stretched further?

And, you know, I always tell people, if I tell you Portugal is cheaper than Ireland but your travel beams are taking you to Ireland, and Ireland is one of the most expensive corners of Europe, your best value is in Ireland, and you should just know how to travel smartly in Ireland.
A smart traveler in one of the more expensive countries can travel, I think, impressively reasonably, but you just have to know how to order lower on the menu.
And I really believe you don't - we don't compromise, in the way of experience, by ordering lower on the menu.
You just surround yourself with local people and actually a more real experience.
But you'd best to throw away that copy of Germany on $5 a day.
Yeah.
It's kind of tough these days.
You know, the Americans have to realize that we're not as rich as we think we are.
We're certainly not poor, but the days that we hop into a cab as effortlessly as the Germans are behind us.
Now, we need to get used to standing in line at the bus stop with the Portuguese people.
You know, we can still have a lot of fun.
Twelve million Americans go to Europe every year.
I'm heading off next Sunday.
I'll be over there for two months and checking all the information in my guide books and, you know, we just need to know how to order smartly.
You know, you can take a shuttle in from any airport in Europe for $15 today.
That's not going to break the bank.
You just don't want to pay $100 for the taxi ride.
You can go to a - instead of a three-star hotel for $300, you go to a one-star hotel for $150 for a double, including breakfast.
Now, you know, in a lot of ways, I think Europe is less expensive than traveling in the United States, even considering our bad dollar.
But, again, this is the same thing I've been preaching for 30 years: You got to travel like a temporary European.
You got to know the alternatives.
Every country has bed and breakfast offering double the cultural intimacy for half the price of a hotel if you know the local word for bed and breakfast.
It is husrim(ph) in Norway and domatio(ph) in Greece and chambre d'hotes in France.
So there's beautiful opportunities for us to travel closer to the ground experience more and spend less, but you got to expect yourself to travel smart.
Yes, I know.
Yeah.
I've spent a third of my adult life, literally, in one-star hotels in Europe, and I've never picked up a fungus.
I made a lot of friends.
Sorry to be so graphic but I'm just telling you, Americans got to learn to just order lower on the menu.
They're good enough for Greeks and Irish and Danes and German students and they got to be good enough for us.
Now, if you've got a lot of money, it's more fun to spend it over there.
I've just been trying to answer this question for a lot of our travelers by putting a chart together.
I'm just looking at it right now.
We'll have it on our blog, I think, on your blog there afterwards.
Oh, that's great.

Yeah, but, I mean, I'm looking at the cost - relative cost of one-star hotel with breakfast anywhere in Europe, and it ranges from $80 in Madrid and Lisbon for the double, to $130 in Rome and Paris and London.
A nicer hotel - in a two-star hotel would range from a 120 to $200.
And what I consider a splurge would be in the $200 to $250 range depending on the country.

But, you know, you get - if you're going to go for the high-rise, American-style hotel that you'd expect to find in Orlando, you're going to - and if you're not a savvy consumer, you're going to pay a fortune and you're going to, ironically, isolate yourself from what you traveled to see.
Again, I'm going to - I'm going to Lisbon next Sunday, and I'm going to spend a $100 a night for my hotel room.
And it's going to be right in the midst of one of my favorite cities in Europe.
And, again, it's - we got to be on the ball, but we got to realize that the experience has very little to do with how much you're spending.
It's just important for us not to be suckered into these strange little worlds where every - like you said, a $10 cup of coffee earlier.
Well, if somebody is spending $10 for a cup of coffee, they're renting real estate, and they better sit there for a couple of hours and watch the view.
(Soundbite of laughter)
I was just in Rome a week ago with my son who's studying there.
And in the tourist area, I was spending $2 for a cup coffee.
And when I went out where my son was staying, where the tourists don't go, it cost $1 for a cup of coffee.
That's with today's exchange rate.

So, you know, it's fun news here in the media to talk about the scary stories, but the reality is there are suckers spending $10 for a cup of coffee or wealthy people that want to enjoy the view or actually, I think, $10 per cup of coffee on St. Mark's Square in Venice is a good deal.
But if you just want just a nice espresso, stand at the bar and you get it for $1 50 yards away.
I was about to feel really bad until I realized they did have that $10 cup of coffee on St. Mark's Square in Venice.
Is that right?
Yeah, but, you see, you're probably paying for the orchestra too and…
Oh, yeah, the orchestra.
Well, the other thing is a lot of people get the bill at a place like that in Venice and say, oh, well, I got to add 20 percent.
Yeah, and that's another point.
I've just written an article called ""Take Back 20,"" talking about 20 ways Americans can take back the 20 percent that our dollar has lost in the last year on the European euro.
And one of the things is: Don't over tip.
American's think we should tip, but in Europe it's a different standard.
And in many cases, service is included in the bill and American's are almost embarrassingly clueless when it comes to tipping.
You want to tip appropriate or when appropriate, but generally in Europe, it is included.
Let's get some listeners in on the conversation.
Our guest, of course, is Rick Steves.
You see him on public television, listen to him on public radio.
Here's your chance to ask him a question about how to travel cheaply in Europe.
E-mail us: talk@npr.org.
Let's begin with Cris(ph).
Cris, calling from Roanoke in Virginia.
Hi.
Hi, there.
Well, thank you for having me on the show.
I kind of wish you had this a few months ago.
My husband and I actually just came back from London and Paris.
And did it break the bank?
No, not really.
Huh, what did you do?
My husband - we thought it would.
We actually have the Rick Steves' guides for both London and Paris.
And we used those, and (unintelligible).
But what we did for London - but went whole to a lot of the free museums.
And there are a lot of free museums in London.
Mm-hmm.
Which was your favorite?
Oh, probably the British Library.
Hmm.
You don't think…
You know…
You don't think of that that as a museum, but it's a great place to hang around for a day or two, Rick?
Neal, I was just - I was thinking Cris was saying London didn't break the bank and I thought, well, Cris is probably enjoying the cultural attractions in London.

London is expensive from a hotel and eating point of view, but you can picnic for the cost of groceries anywhere in the world, obviously, and then you get out there and you see these cultural wonders.
I wonder if you, as a parent, would want to know?
Rami Khouri, after the conflict ended, there were all kinds of pledges of support from many nations around the world, particularly in the Middle East, for reconstruction aid for Lebanon.
Hezbollah wanted to do a lot of the reconstruction itself.
The United States was providing funds.
How has that gone?

National Gallery, free; Changing of the Guard every day at 11:30, free; lunch at St. Martins in the Fields, 1:00, free; British Museum, free; British Library, free; Evensong at St. Paul's Cathedral, normally $15 to get in, but free for the Evensong service daily at 5 o'clock; and then you go to a carnival-like scene in Covent Gardens and Piccadilly Circus.

Next day, Portobello Road Market, free; go to the auction at Sotheby's free; Victoria and Albert Museum, free; stroll the Jubilee Promenade along the south bank of the Thames; stop at Shakespeare's Globe Theater for a play; groundling tickets with your elbows on the stage costs 5 pounds.
That's $10 to see some of the greatest Shakespeare in the world.
So those are budget ways to do the most expensive city in Europe.

So you have that $300 hotel, but if you don't mind using the tube, equipping yourself with good information and expecting yourself to travel smart like Cris did, the rest of it can be an incredibly good value, and talk about experience.
